extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('p') (line 39) is passed directly to os.path.join(base_path, filename) and opened with open(). No sanitization or validation is performed on the filename, allowing an attacker to use path traversal sequences (e.g. '../../') to read arbitrary files on the filesystem.

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')

extra.message: User input from request.POST.get('first_name') (line 68) is base64-decoded and passed directly to exec(), allowing arbitrary Python code execution on the server. An attacker can craft a base64-encoded payload that executes any command, such as writing files or spawning a reverse shell.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')

extra.message: User input from request.POST.get('first_name') is base64-decoded and passed to exec() in the Python 2 fallback path. This is the same code injection vulnerability as line 72 but for Python 2 compatibility, allowing arbitrary code execution.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('qs') is rendered in a JavaScript context using the |safe filter: var myvar = "{{qs|safe}}";. The |safe filter disables Django's auto-escaping, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary JavaScript by crafting a query string that breaks out of the string literal. This is a reflected XSS vulnerability.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('qs') is rendered inside an HTML attribute using the |safe filter: value="{{qs|safe}}". The |safe filter disables Django's auto-escaping, allowing an attacker to break out of the attribute and inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript. This is a reflected XSS vulnerability.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User input from the URL path parameter is rendered in a JavaScript context using the |safe filter: var myvar = "{{path|safe}}";. The |safe filter disables Django's auto-escaping, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary JavaScript by manipulating the URL path. This is a reflected XSS vulnerability.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('qs') is rendered in a JavaScript context using the |safe filter: var x = "{{qs|safe}}";. The |safe filter disables Django's auto-escaping, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary JavaScript via the query string. This is a reflected XSS vulnerability.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: The dor_user_profile view allows accessing and modifying any user's profile by changing the userid URL parameter. There is no authentication or authorization check to verify that the requesting user is authorized to view or modify the requested profile. Additionally, on POST requests (lines 132-135), user data can be modified (name and email) without any authorization, allowing an attacker to modify other users' data.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The missing_access_control view grants access to admin functionality based solely on a query parameter (?action=admin). There is no authentication or authorization check — any user can access the admin page by adding ?action=admin to the URL.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('url') is passed directly to Django's redirect() without any validation or allowlist. An attacker can craft a URL that redirects users to an arbitrary external domain, enabling phishing attacks.

cwe: CWE-601: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('fwd') is used to look up a function in globals() and then call it: function = globals().get(forward); if function: return function(request). An attacker can call any module-level function by name, including dangerous built-in functions or other view functions (e.g., fwd=code_execution, fwd=admin), bypassing intended access controls.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')

extra.message: The @csrf_exempt decorator on csrf_image disables Django's CSRF protection for this view. Combined with the global CSRF middleware being commented out in settings.py (line 102), this view is completely unprotected against CSRF attacks.

The Django CSRF middleware (django.middleware.csrf.CsrfViewMiddleware) is commented out in MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES, disabling CSRF protection for the entire application. This makes all state-changing requests vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks.

The Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in the source code: SECRET_KEY = 'h++jszpm)i@p%ay_b=cp#()^od!qns14)h%@qm3)p=cuo+st^a'. This key is used for cryptographic signing (session cookies, password reset tokens, CSRF tokens, etc.). Exposure of this key allows an attacker to forge sessions, bypass CSRF protection, and generate valid password reset tokens.

DEBUG = True is set in settings.py. In production, this exposes detailed error pages with stack traces, local variables, settings (including SECRET_KEY), and SQL queries. This can leak sensitive information to attackers. Combined with the boom() view that intentionally raises exceptions (views.py:144), this guarantees that detailed debug information is exposed.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
